text: Summation
Persuade
Reasonable doubt
Rebut
Weigh
Inference
Deliberate
Plead
Undermine
Compelling
Cumulative
Reiterate
It was the prosecution that promised certainty.
What the evidence shows is a reasonable doubt.
Not once did the witness mention a weapon.
Never in this trial have we seen direct proof.
Counsel, you have ten minutes. Address the jury with your closing.
Thank you, Your Honor. Members of the jury, it was the prosecution that promised certainty and what they gave you is a reasonable doubt.
I ask you to weigh the evidence honestly. Not once did a single witness place my client at the scene.
Let me rebut their strongest point. The only fair inference from these facts is that someone else acted that night.
Counsel, keep to the evidence on the record.
Of course. Let me reiterate the one theme of this case. What you must decide is not whether you dislike the crime, but whether the proof is compelling.
I do not plead for sympathy. I plead for a fair reading of the facts when you deliberate.
Thank you, counsel. The jury will retire to deliberate.

Before you deliver your first summation, let me tell you what truly persuades a jury. It is not volume, and it is not theatrics. What wins a closing argument is structure. Begin by reminding the jury of the burden: the prosecution must prove its case beyond a reasonable doubt, and you only have to show that the doubt is reasonable. Then weigh the evidence piece by piece, and draw the inference that favors your client. Rebut the strongest point the other side made, never the weakest. Reiterate one clear theme, three or four times, until the jury can repeat it in the deliberation room. And close with a plea that is calm and human: what you ask of them is not sympathy, but a fair reading of the facts. It is the lawyer who keeps it simple who is most compelling.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. The trial of the State versus Andrade reached its closing arguments today, and the courtroom was full. In her summation, the prosecutor argued that the evidence was cumulative and compelling. But it was the defense that controlled the room. Not once did counsel raise his voice. What he did instead was weigh each fact aloud and ask the jury to draw a different inference. He reminded them that it is reasonable doubt, not certainty, that decides the case, and he undermined the central witness with a single quiet question. The jury will begin to deliberate tomorrow morning. Whatever the verdict, observers agreed that the closing was a model of restraint. We will follow the case as it develops.
